coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional selenium-doped carbon dots for modulating Alzheimer's disease related toxic ions, inhibiting amyloid aggregation and scavenging reactive oxygen species
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Shao, Xu
Fan, Tiange
Yan, Chaoren
Cao, Xiuyun
Wang, Chao
Wang, Xin
Guan, Ping
Fan, Li
Hu, Xiaoling
coredata.dc:description: β-Amyloid (Aβ) protein deposition, oxidative stress, and metal ion imbalance are established pathological features of Alzheimer's disease (AD), highlighting the imperative to efficiently reduce Aβ aggregates formation, alleviate oxidative stress, and chelate metal ions. Existing research indicates the necessity of developing multifunctional nanomaterials to facilitate multi-target therapy. In this work, we designed and prepared multifunctional selenium-doped carbonized polymer dots (SeCDs), and examined the multifunctionality at inhibiting Aβ, cleaning reactive oxygen species (ROS), and modulating copper ions. SeCDs exhibit efficient clearance of active hydroxyl radicals and superoxide anion radicals. In addition, SeCDs can chelate Cu ions, therefore reducing the cytotoxicity linked to the Aβ-Cu complex. More importantly, SeCDs can effectively reduce the level of intracellular reactive oxygen species. This study demonstrates the potential of carbon dots as a multifunctional β-sheet disruptor, while multifunctional SeCDs offer promising avenues for further research in the multi-target treatment of Alzheimer's disease. Meanwhile, this strategy provides a new perspective on the development of zero-dimensional carbon materials in Alzheimer's therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
ROS elimination
Anti-aggregation
Ion chelation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: SeCDs interferes with Aβ-aggregation and alleviates Aβ-Cu-mediated cytotoxicity
The effect of SeCDs in scavenging ROS
Determination of chelating ability of copper ion

This work was financially supported by the Key Research and Development Program of Shaanxi ( 2022GY-198 , and 2023-YBGY-295 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images of SeCDs. (B) Histogram showing size distribution. (C) AFM imaging of SeCDs. (D) AFM height dispersion. (E-J) SeCDs XPS complete spectrum with high-resolution C1s, Se3d, S2p, N1s and O1s. (K) FT-IR spectrum of SeCDs. (L) UV–Vis absorption spectra of SeCDs.

(A) Excitation-emission matrix fluorescence spectrum of SeCDs. (B) Emission spectra corresponding to various excitation wavelengths. (C) Excitation spectra associated with distinct emission wavelengths. (D) Two-dimensional fluorescence spectra of SeCDs.

(A) Fluorescence quenching capacity of SeCDs by various metal ions. (B) Fluorescence alterations of SeCDs after interaction with various metal ions. (C) Effect of Cu2+ at varying concentrations on the fluorescence quenching of SeCDs. (D) Linear correlation between the fluorescence intensity of SeCDs and the concentration of Cu2+. (E) SeCDs compete with BCA to amalgamate Cu+. (F) Linear correlation between the absorbance intensity of Cu+(BCA)2 at 562 nm and the concentration of SeCDs.

(A) The absorption spectrums of DPPH after 10 min of the reaction with different concentrations of SeCDs. (B) The SeCDs concentration-dependent elimination efficiencies of DPPH. (C) The kinetic curves of DPPH reduction at different SeCDs concentrations. (D) Temporal variation of the MB absorption peak upon the addition of 25 μg·mL−1 SeCDs. (E) MB absorption peak at 660 nm with varying doses SeCDs after 600 s. (F) The efficacy of •OH elimination by SeCDs is dependent on concentration. (G) The kinetic curves of •OH reduction at different SeCDs concentrations. (H) The absorption spectrums of formazan after 5 min of the reaction with different concentrations of SeCDs. (I) The SeCDs concentration-dependent elimination efficiencies of •O2 −.

(A) ThT fluorescence assay of Aβ42 (20 μM) in the absence or presence of SeCDs and Cu2+ (SeCDs: 10 μg·mL−1; Cu2+: 300 μM). (B) Corresponding inhibition efficiency of SeCDs for Aβ42 fibrillation. (C) ThT fluorescence assay of Aβ42 fibrils in the absence or presence of SeCDs and Cu2+ (SeCDs: 10 μg·mL−1; Cu2+: 300 μM). (D) Corresponding disaggregation efficiency of SeCDs for Aβ42 fibrils. (E) Circular dichroism spectra of Aβ42 cultivated for 48 h with various treatments. (F) Tyrosine inner fluorescence assay of Aβ42.

(A) SeCDs at varying doses affect HT22 cell activity. (B) Toxicity of Aβ-Cu2+ to HT22 cells (Aβ: μM, Cu2+: μM). (C) SeCDs alleviate the toxicity of Aβ to HT22 cells (Aβ: μM, SeCDs: μg·mL−1). (D) SeCDs alleviate the toxicity of Aβ-Cu2+ to HT22 cells (Aβ: μM, Cu2+: μM, SeCDs: μg·mL−1).

ROS scavenging activity of SeCDs in HT22 cells. (A) Quantitative analysis by flow cytometry. (B) The CLSM images of HT22 cells. The ROS-positive cells show green fluorescence. The nuclei were stained with DAPI (blue fluorescence). (C) The CLSM images of HT22 cells. The ROS-positive cells show red fluorescence. The nuclei were stained with DAPI (blue fluorescence). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(A) Synthesize of SeCDs. (B) SeCDs chelate copper ions and scavenge reactive oxygen species for synergistic Alzheimer's disease treatment.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional selenium-doped carbon dots for modulating Alzheimer's disease related toxic ions, inhibiting amyloid aggregation and scavenging reactive oxygen species

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry and Analysis
Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Chiral Drug and Vaccine Adjuvants

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry and Analysis, School of Pharmacy, Air Force Medical University, Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Chiral Drug and Vaccine Adjuvants, 169 Changle West Road, Xi'an 710032, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Xu Shao and Tiange Fan contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: β-Amyloid (Aβ) protein deposition, oxidative stress, and metal ion imbalance are established pathological features of Alzheimer's disease (AD), highlighting the imperative to efficiently reduce Aβ aggregates formation, alleviate oxidative stress, and chelate metal ions. Existing research indicates the necessity of developing multifunctional nanomaterials to facilitate multi-target therapy. In this work, we designed and prepared multifunctional selenium-doped carbonized polymer dots (SeCDs), and examined the multifunctionality at inhibiting Aβ, cleaning reactive oxygen species (ROS), and modulating copper ions. SeCDs exhibit efficient clearance of active hydroxyl radicals and superoxide anion radicals. In addition, SeCDs can chelate Cu ions, therefore reducing the cytotoxicity linked to the Aβ-Cu complex. More importantly, SeCDs can effectively reduce the level of intracellular reactive oxygen species. This study demonstrates the potential of carbon dots as a multifunctional β-sheet disruptor, while multifunctional SeCDs offer promising avenues for further research in the multi-target treatment of Alzheimer's disease. Meanwhile, this strategy provides a new perspective on the development of zero-dimensional carbon materials in Alzheimer's therapy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Considered an incurable disease, Alzheimer's disease (AD) is a prevalent neurodegenerative ailment that affects cognitive function, results in significant neurological abnormalities, and causes mobility dysfunction [1–3]. Alzheimer's disease has persistently presented problems for mankind, and its pathogenic mechanism is notably complex [4]. Nowadays, three widely acknowledged hypotheses are interrelated, which include amyloid cascade hypothesis, oxidative stress hypothesis, and metal ion imbalance hypothesis [5,6]. In amyloid cascade hypothesis, the self-assembly of Aβ monomers into aggregates rich in β-sheets and their extracellular accumulation might result in the initial signs of Alzheimer's disease, such as neuronal cell death, synaptic dysfunction, and cognitive decline [7,8]. The Aβ peptide can enhance the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and trigger oxidative stress. Oxidative stress induces cellular damage and mitochondrial dysfunction, subsequently prompting immune cells to activate and exacerbate the inflammatory response. Slowing or treating AD may benefit from reducing oxidative stress [9–11]. In metal ion imbalance hypothesis, a lot of studies have demonstrated increased levels of metal ions (such as copper, zinc, and iron) in amyloid plaques, suggesting a relationship between metal ions and beta-amyloidosis [12–14]. Aβ accumulates in the brain, facilitated by metal ions, especially copper ions, and contributes to the production of reactive oxygen species [12]. 3–5 metal ions interact with a single pair of Aβ residues, such as histidine (His), forming an Aβ-metal complex that influences the aggregation kinetics of Aβ [13]. Cu2+ has attracted considerable attention due to its high binding constant with Aβ. The Aβ-Cu2+ complex produces and stabilizes oligomers, which represent the primary toxic Aβ species [15]. Moreover, the REDOX activities of Cu2+ and Cu+ induce oxidative stress in the Fenton process, hence exacerbating Aβ-mediated cytotoxicity.

Nanomaterials provide substantial benefits in the treatment of neurodegenerative disorder [5,16]. Carbon dots are a novel material with facile preparation and easy raw material acquisition, and Controllable carbon dots synthesis and use have been studied extensively [17,18]. Carbon dots represent a new class of quasi-spherical carbon-based quantum nanoparticles, distinguished by unique physicochemical and optical properties, a high surface-to-volume ratio, surface tunability, and excellent biocompatibility, positioning them as promising candidates for therapeutic applications [19,20]. Carbon dots-based nanomaterials are widely employed as nanomedicines or nanocarriers for overcoming the blood-brain barrier and inhibiting amyloid aggregate [21–23]. Numerous studies have shown that carbon dots are used in the diagnosis and treatment of Alzheimer's disease [24,25]. Our recent research has shown that carbon dots may transport medications across the blood-brain barrier and effectively prevent Aβ fibrosis and the carbon dots themselves can be used as nanomedicine [26–28].

Glutathione comprises glutamic acid, cysteine, and glycine, and has a sulfhydryl group. It is essential for maintaining cellular biological functions and has several biochemical roles, especially good antioxidant properties [29]. Selenium is a crucial trace element for humans, with the capacity to resist oxidation and chelate copper ions [30,31]. The copper ion is closely linked to the etiology of Alzheimer's disease. We synthesized novel carbon dots SeCDs, using glutathione and L-selenomethionine as precursors to effectively bind to the copper ion-bound Aβ complex and confer antioxidant capabilities. SeCDs may eradicate reactive oxygen species, inhibit amyloid-β aggregation, and chelate copper ions. This work demonstrates the potential of carbon dots as a multifunctional β-sheet disruptor and presents a feasible anti-amyloidosis strategy for future Alzheimer's disease therapies. Scheme 1A illustrates the synthesis method for SeCDs, whereas Scheme 1B demonstrates their effects.

The hydrothermal method used glutathione and L-selenomethionine as precursors, with an initial investigation into the optimal ratio of precursors for carbon dots synthesis. One carbon dot was synthesized using glutathione as the sole precursor. And three carbon dots were synthesized by glutathione: L-selenomethionine mass ratio of 2:1, 1:1, and 1:2. Fig. S1A presents the photoelectron spectra for various carbon dots. The increase in the L-selenomethionine ratio in the precursor correlates with a gradual rise in the Se element content in the carbon dots. Proceed with the infrared characterization of carbon dots, as illustrated in Fig. S1B. The absorption peak at approximately 2600 cm−1 corresponds to the -SH stretching vibration. The peaks at 1600 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1 are associated with C=O/C=C stretching vibrations. The range of 1000–1200 cm−1 indicates CS stretching vibrations, while the peak at 761 cm−1 is attributed to CSe stretching vibrations [32]. A preliminary in vitro study was conducted to investigate the antioxidant properties of carbon dots with varying selenium content, utilizing 2,2-Diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) and •OH as research models, as illustrated in Fig. S2. The results indicate that both exhibited similar outcomes, specifically that the antioxidant capacity increased with a higher proportion of glutathione in the precursor. To further ascertain the optimal precursor ratio, the binding capacity of Cu2+ to doped carbon dots with varying Se contents was investigated, as illustrated in Fig. S3. The introduction of Se element significantly enhanced the binding ability of carbon dots to Cu2+. To ensure both oxidation resistance and chelating ability of carbon dots, we selected the carbon dots with a precursor ratio of 1:1 for further research, designated as SeCDs.

SeCDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), as illustrated in Fig. 1A. The findings indicate that the particle size is consistent, approximately 3 nm. SeCDs were further characterized using atomic force microscopy (AFM), as illustrated in Fig. 1C. The AFM image indicates that the height of SeCDs ranges from 2.0 to 3.0 nm, suggesting a spherical morphology for SeCDs (Fig. 1D). X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS) was employed to analyze the structure and elemental composition of the SeCDs. Five distinct peaks were identified in the XPS spectra corresponding to C1s, N1s, O1s, Se3d, and S2p at 284, 400, 531, 55, and 165 eV, respectively (Fig. 1e) [33]. Fig. 1F–J presents a high-resolution map of the corresponding elements, indicating that SeCDs contain CSe, CS, SO, CN, and other chemical bonds. The C1s sub-peak spectra of SeCDs exhibits absorption peaks at 284.8, 287.5, and 289.7 eV, corresponding to CC, C-O/C-N/C-Se, and CO, respectively. SeCDs have two distinct peaks at 399.1 eV and 401.6 eV, which correspond to NH and C-N-C, respectively. SeCDs exhibit two different peaks at 528.9 eV and 530.8 eV, which correspond to CO and CO, respectively. The S 2p peak spectrogram of SeCDs exhibits two distinct peaks at 163.4 eV and 168.7 eV, which correspond to CS and SO, respectively. The distinctive peak at 54.1 eV corresponds to CSe in the high-resolution Se 3d spectra of SeCDs, signifying the presence of CSe, CS, SO, CN, and other chemical bonds, hence confirming the effective incorporation of selenium into the SeCDs. SeCDs were further characterized by FT-IR, as shown in Fig. 1K. The CSe absorption peak at 761 cm−1 signifies the presence of Se, while the peak at 1672 cm−1 corresponds to the CO vibration. Additionally, the weak absorption peak near 2500 cm−1 suggests the potential presence of -SH groups on the SeCDs surface, and the peak at 1416 cm−1 represents the CH stretching vibration. The peak observed at 1000–1200 cm−1 corresponds to the CS vibration. The UV absorption peak at approximately 270 nm corresponds to the π → π* transition associated with the SeCDs conjugate structure (Fig. 1L).

We investigated the fluorescence properties of SeCDs by measuring the excitation-emission matrix fluorescence spectrum, utilizing an excitation range of 200–900 nm and an emission range of 200–900 nm. Fig. 2A illustrates the three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of SeCDs, which display a prominent bright center. Fig. 2B illustrates the photoluminescence emission of the SeCDs solution across various excitation wavelengths. Fig. 2C demonstrates the photoluminescent excitation of the SeCDs solution across different emission wavelengths. Fig. 2D illustrates that the optimal excitation wavelength for SeCDs is 350 nm, while the optimal emission wavelength is 430 nm, as indicated by the fluorescence excitation and emission spectrum. Fluorescence quenching refers to the alteration in fluorescence intensity or related excitation or fluorescence peaks between fluorescent molecules and solvent molecules. Given the notable alteration in the emission spectra peaks at different excitation wavelengths, we select the excitation pattern of SeCDs for the subsequent fluorescence quenching studies.

Copper ions facilitate Aβ aggregation, leading to Aβ misfolding and the formation of oligomers, whereas Aβ-Cu2+ complexes alter metalloenzyme function and enhance ROS generation [34]. The chelating capacity of SeCDs for various metal ions was investigated. Fig. 3A and B illustrate that the assessment of the binding affinity between SeCDs and Cu2+ mostly relied on variations in the fluorescence signal. Among prevalent metal ions, Cu2+ has the most potent quenching capability for SeCDs. Fig. 3C illustrates that the fluorescence intensity of SeCDs progressively diminishes when the concentration of Cu2+ varies between 0 and 100 μM, signifying an interaction between Cu2+ and SeCDs. For instance, it interacts with amine nitrogen and carboxylate oxygen atoms, as well as other surface groups or selenium in selenium carbon dots, to create tridentate compounds, hence influencing its fluorescence properties [35]. Fig. 3D illustrates a strong linear correlation between the fluorescence intensity of SeCDs and the concentration of Cu2+. This data indicates that Cu2+ has a robust interaction with SeCDs.

Simultaneously, several studies have demonstrated that the REDOX mechanism involving Cu+ and Cu2+ facilitates the generation of substantial quantities of reactive oxygen species (ROS), with Cu+ exhibiting a greater and more deleterious binding affinity for Aβ [36]. In the presence of oxygen, H2O2 may be synthesized by the reaction of Cu+/Aβ species. In Fenton-like chemistry, Cu+/Aβ may also convert H2O2 into hydroxyl radicals (•OH). This results in the additional creation of extra radicals. Disodium 2,2-biquinoline-4,4-dicarboxylate (BCA) and Cu+ create a stable purple complex Cu+(BCA)2 in a 2:1 ratio, exhibiting maximum absorbance at 562 nm. The reduction in UV–vis absorbance of Cu+(BCA)2 at 562 nm might indicate the transfer of Cu+ from BCA. Fig. 3E illustrates that when the concentration of SeCDs rises, the absorbance at 562 nm diminishes, suggesting that SeCDs competitively bind to Cu+. The binding energy of SeCDs to Cu+ surpasses that of BCA, potentially attributable to the coordination of CuSe and Cu-OCO interactions [37]. Fig. 3F demonstrates that the absorbance of Cu+(BCA)2 at 562 nm exhibits a strong linear correlation with the concentration of SeCDs. The results demonstrate that SeCDs exhibit effective chelation of Cu2+ and Cu+ ions.

The DPPH radical was chosen as the model to assess the RONS scavenging activity of the synthesized SeCDs. DPPH, being an N-centered free radical, has a distinct absorption peak at 515 nm [38]. Upon interaction with free radical scavengers, the DPPH peak diminishes because of DPPH complex formation. The elimination of DPPH represents the anti-ROS ability. As shown in Fig. 4A, the peak intensity at 517 nm displays a continuous decline as the concentration of SeCDs increased from 0 to 200 μg·mL−1. Meanwhile, the characteristic purple color of the DPPH solution gradually faded with the increasing amount of SeCDs added. As seen in Fig. 4B, at a SeCDs concentration of 200 μg·mL−1, the DPPH clearance rate is 69.56 %. The clearing balance may be attained within 200 s, as seen in Fig. 4C. These results indicate the removal of DPPH. Electron-withdrawing groups on DPPH interact with hydroxyl, amino, or sulfhydryl groups on the surface of SeCDs, resulting in the contraction of the conjugated system. Alternatively, sp2 carbon nuclei and electron-donating doping sites can eliminate free radicals via addition reactions and spin delocalization of the conjugated graphene backbone [39].

Hydroxyl radicals are potent oxidative free radicals that can cause degradation of cell membranes and DNA, greatly contributing to oxidative damage [40]. The •OH scavenging ability of SeCDs was systematically measured utilizing methylene blue (MB) as a probe, whose absorption peak decreases at 660 nm in the presence of •OH [41]. The classic Fe2+/H2O2 Fenton reaction was utilized to generate •OH in situ in aqueous solution. Introduce varying quantities of SeCDs, allow a 5-minute interval, then incorporate the MB indicator. Fig. 4D illustrates the temporal variation of the MB absorption peak at a SeCDs concentration of 25 μg·mL−1. Fig. 4E illustrates the UV absorption peak of MB with varying doses SeCDs after 600 s. The UV absorbance peak of MB rises with increasing SeCDs concentration, suggesting that SeCDs diminish •OH levels, hence mitigating the oxidation of MB by •OH. The color alteration of MB in Fig. S4 demonstrates the same result. At a concentration of SeCDs of 100 μg·mL−1, the scavenging effectiveness of •OH was 75.11 % (Fig. 4F). Fig. 4G illustrates the kinetic profiles of MB at 660 nm with varying doses SeCDs adding. The results demonstrate that SeCDs possess a remarkable capacity to eliminate •OH.

Alzheimer's disease patients generate excessive superoxide anions in the brain, resulting in oxidative stress and neuronal and glial cell damage [42]. Nitro blue tetrazolium chloride (NBT) was utilized as an indicator to measure SeCDs' superoxide dismutase (SOD)-like activity against •O2 – [43]. NBT is a sensitive superoxide indicator, that can be reduced via •O2 − and form a blue formazan compound. In the presence of SeCDs, the absorbance value at 560 nm decreases dramatically with increasing concentration of SeCDs, attributed to the dismutation of •O2 − by the SeCDs (Fig. 4H). The elimination efficiency of •O2 − was approximately 90 % at a concentration of 100 μg·mL−1 (Fig. 4I). The results demonstrate that SeCDs have superoxide dismutase-like activity. Besides the impact of functional groups of the SeCDs surface, the Se doping site may serve as the active site for the elimination of hydroxyl radicals and superoxide anions by facilitating electron transfer to the hybrid carbon's main chain [44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: SeCDs interferes with Aβ-aggregation and alleviates Aβ-Cu-mediated cytotoxicity
ce:section.ce:para: Aβ42 is a crucial factor in the development of AD. In the presence of metal ions like Cu2+, Aβ42 exhibits an increased propensity for aggregation, with Aβ-Cu2+ demonstrating heightened toxicity and inducing greater oxidative stress [45]. Therefore, we investigated the ability of SeCDs to inhibit Aβ42 fibrillation and disaggregate mature Aβ42 fibrils. Fig. 5A shows that ThT fluorescence was highest when Aβ42 was co-grown with Cu2+, and Aβ42 fluorescence was strong when cultured alone. When both systems were co-cultured with 10 μg·mL−1 SeCDs, ThT fluorescence diminished. Fig. 5B shows that co-incubation of Aβ42 and Cu2+ led to a 42.1 % rise in ThT fluorescence intensity. Adding SeCDs reduced the ThT fluorescence intensity of Aβ42 incubated with Cu2+ by 48.9 %, whereas Aβ42 incubated with SeCDs dropped by 50.9 %. Fig. 5C displays the findings of further investigation into the disaggregation effect of SeCDs on the Aβ42 fibrils. When the fibrils were co-cultured with Cu2+, the fluorescence intensity of ThT was highest. ThT fluorescence intensity in the Aβ fibrils group remained at a high level, but in the SeCDs group it dropped. Fig. 5D shows that co-incubation with Cu2+ raised ThT fluorescence intensity by 31.4 % compared to the Aβ42 fibrils group, lowered it by 46.4 % when co-cultured with SeCDs. ThT fluorescence intensity decreased by 47.3 % when Aβ42 co-cultured with SeCDs. Fig. 5E displays the circular dichroism spectrum of Aβ42 monomer after 48 h of different treatments. The Aβ-Cu2+ group has a negative peak at 216 nm, suggesting the presence of more β-sheet structures in the secondary structure [26]. The Aβ group's negative peak at 216 nm is weaker towards Aβ-Cu2+ group. SeCDs diminish the negative peak of the Aβ group and Aβ-Cu2+ group. Cu2+ increases the hazardous β-sheet structure of Aβ42 monomer, leading to severe aggregation. The addition of SeCDs reduces the β-sheet content in Aβ42, demonstrating that SeCDs can prevent Cu2+-induced amyloid protein misfolding. The circular dichroism spectrum data indicate the contents of α-helix and β-sheet in Aβ following various treatments, as presented in Table S1. When SeCDs were added, the content of β-sheet decreased.

The tyrosine fluorescence of Aβ42 was employed to further elucidate the relationship between SeCDs and Aβ42. Tyrosine fluorescence, the endogenous fluorescence of Aβ42, may be used to evaluate Aβ42 adsorption or binding by measuring the degree of quenching [46]. Fig. 5F shows that when the concentration of SeCDs increased, the endogenous fluorescence of tyrosine decreased. This suggests that SeCDs bind Aβ by hydrophobic interaction and π-π stacking. This finding supports the affinity of SeCDs for Aβ42.

The cytotoxicity of SeCDs to HT22 cells at several dosages was examined, revealing that SeCDs exhibited little toxicity to HT22 cells. At a SeCDs concentration of 200 μg·mL−1, cell viability was measured at 86.21 % (Fig. 6A). The hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining of major organs of administered mice also indicated that SeCDs have good biocompatibility (Fig. S7). The effects of Aβ, copper ions, and their concurrent presence on HT22 cells were further examined. At a copper ion concentration of 20 μM, the activity of HT22 cells is 90.96 %, whereas at 300 μM, the activity declines to 60.80 %. This demonstrates significant cytotoxicity of copper ions to neuronal cells, with the concentration in the brains of Alzheimer's disease patients approximating 300 μM [47,48]. Upon the addition of 20 μM Aβ, the activity of HT22 cells was measured at 83.26 %, signifying that Aβ disrupted the physiological functions of normal cells throughout the oligomerization process. Upon the addition of both copper ions and Aβ at a concentration of 20 μM, HT22 cell viability diminished to 68.95 %. The addition of 20 μM Aβ and 300 μM copper ions resulted in cell activity of just 51.94 %, demonstrating the significant toxicity of Aβ and copper ions on neuronal cells (Fig. 6B). Fig. 6C shows the ability of SeCDs to alleviate Aβ-mediated cytotoxicity, and it can be found that with the increase of SeCDs concentration, the cell activity is higher. The efficacy of various doses of SeCDs in mitigating the cytotoxicity of the Aβ-Cu2+ complex was investigated. Fig. 6D illustrates that the addition of SeCDs at a concentration of 5 μg·mL−1 elevated cell activity from 51.94 % to 56.34 %. The cellular activity was 60.16 % and 65.95 % upon the addition of SeCDs at a concentration of 10 μg·mL−1 and 25 μg·mL−1. The findings indicate that SeCDs may mitigate the toxicity of the Aβ-Cu2+ combination to neuronal cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of SeCDs in scavenging ROS
ce:section.ce:para: Overproduction of ROS can lead to neurological dysfunction and induction of cellular apoptosis and necrosis, contributing crucially to the pathogenesis of AD [49]. Consequently, the removal of excess ROS could serve as an effective treatment for AD. Intracellular ROS scavenging activity of SeCDs was evaluated with HT22 cells by DCFH-DA assay [50]. The changes of intracellular total ROS mediated by Aβ42 were quantitatively analyzed by flow cytometry (Fig. 7A). In contrast to the empty control group, the ROS concentration of HT22 cells treated with Aβ42 exhibited a significant increase of 48.8 %. After 5, 10 and 25 μg·mL−1 SeCDs treatment, the level of ROS was reduced to 43.8, 31.8 and 24.0 %, respectively. As shown in Fig. 7B, the HT22 cells treated with Aβ42 displayed clear green fluorescence compared to the control group. After the addition of SeCDs, the green fluorescence intensity was significantly reduced, and the green fluorescence gradually disappeared with the increase in the concentration of SeCDs. In Fig. S5, the mean fluorescence intensity of confocal microscope figures from various treated groups is statistically examined, demonstrating the aforesaid findings.

Neurons depend significantly on mitochondria and are susceptible to mitochondrial malfunction because of their intricate structure and elevated metabolic demands. Neuronal dysfunction is the fundamental characteristic of neurodegenerative disorders and is intimately associated with mitochondrial dysfunction [51]. MitoSOX Red is a fluorescent probe for living cells that selectively targets mitochondria and possesses cell membrane permeability [3]. MitoSOX Red is specifically oxidized by superoxides upon entering the mitochondria, rather than by other reactive oxygen species or reactive nitrogen species-producing processes. The oxidized MitoSOX Red subsequently interacts with nucleic acids in the mitochondria and nucleus, resulting in intense red fluorescence. MitoSOX was chosen as the indication for mitochondrial superoxide, as seen in Fig. 7C. In comparison to the control group, the Aβ group had increased red fluorescence, which diminished with the introduction of SeCDs at varying doses, suggesting that SeCDs possess the capacity to eliminate superoxide in mitochondria. Fig. S6 shows a statistical calculation of the mean red fluorescence intensity, which similarly illustrates the findings. This further suggested that SeCDs possess a remarkable capacity to scavenge ROS. Overall, these results collectively demonstrate that SeCDs can effectively eliminate intracellular ROS and further highlight their potential as excellent ROS scavenger.

Endothelial cells are the fundamental elements of the BBB, and they serve critical functions in regulating the flow of chemicals into the brain. CLSM was conducted on the chosen bEnd.3 cells as shown in Fig. S8. The cell membrane was stained with Dio dye and fluoresced green under 488 nm excited, while SeCDs fluoresced blue under 405 nm excited. After adding SeCDs, the blue fluorescence in bEnd.3 cells gradually enhanced with the increase of co-incubation time, which indicates that SeCDs entered bEnd.3 cells via endocytosis. This finding indicates the promising potential of SeCDs in overcoming the BBB and their immense potential in the diagnosis and treatment of AD.

This work utilized glutathione and L-selenomethionine as precursors for the synthesis of SeCDs, which exhibited superior antioxidant properties. SeCDs effectively eliminate reactive oxygen species and demonstrate strong chelation with Cu2+ and Cu+. The binding of SeCDs to Cu2+ can inhibit the misfolding of Aβ42 protein mediated by Cu2+. SeCDs can reduce the toxicity of Aβ-Cu2+ to neuronal cells, eliminate reactive oxygen species generated by Aβ42 aggregation process, and sustain the homeostasis of the cellular growth environment. This study establishes a solid research foundation for the development of novel antioxidant carbon dots and their application in Alzheimer's disease.

L-selenomethionine and glutathione were solubilized in water at a mass ratio of 1:1. After 10 min of ultrasonication, the samples were transferred to a reaction kettle maintained at 180 °C for a duration of 4 h. After reaching room temperature, the samples were filtered through a 0.22-micron filter membrane, placed in a 500 Da dialysis bag for 48 h. The dialysis buffer utilized is deionized water, with a water intake of 2 L each session and a change every 12 h. Finally, freeze-dried 72 h for future use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of chelating ability of copper ion
ce:section.ce:para: BCA reacts with Cu+ in a 2:1 ratio to yield Cu+(BCA)2, resulting in a UV absorption peak at 562 nm; hence, the transfer of Cu+ from BCA protein may be assessed by the UV absorption peak of Cu+(BCA)2 at 562 nm. Introduce 20 μM [Cu(MeCN)4]PF6 and 60 μM BCA, both solubilized in Tris-HCl (50 mM, pH 7.4), then incorporate 1 mM AA to inhibit the oxidation of Cu+. The UV absorbance at 562 nm was assessed by including varying quantities of SeCDs into the prepared solution and allowing the reaction to proceed at room temperature for 2 h.

Fluorescence quenching of SeCDs and Cu2+ takes place. The fluorescence variation of SeCDs was measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer to describe their interaction. After constructing a certain concentration of SeCDs solution and introducing several amounts of Cu2+ (0–100 μM), the alteration in excitation intensity of the solution at about 360 nm was assessed at the emission wavelength of 430 nm.

DPPH free radical scavenging activity: We determined the DPPH scavenging percentage using a calibration curve based on UV–Vis absorbance at 517 nm in the presence of various concentrations of SeCDs. Different concentrations of SeCDs were added to DPPH ethanol solution (0.1 mM, 2 mL, pH = 7). Over a period of 600 s, the UV–Vis absorbance at 517 nm was measured every 100 s.

•OH scavenging activity: Initially, hydroxyl radicals were produced by the Fenton reaction, followed by the assessment of SeCDs' scavenging effectiveness through the measurement of methylene blue's absorption peak at 660 nm. In summary, H2O2 (0.1 mM), ferrous sulphate (0.65 mM), and SeCDs (0–100 μg·mL−1) at several concentrations were reacted in an aqueous solution for 5 min, after which methylene blue solution (1 mM) was introduced to measure the change in absorbance at 660 nm. The scavenging efficacy of •OH is determined as follows: (A1-A2)/(A1-A0) × 100 %, where A0 represents the absorbance of aqueous solutions of H2O2 and ferrous sulphate, A1 denotes the absorbance of the control group, and A2 indicates the absorbance of the dosing group.

•O2 − scavenging activity: Nitro blue tetrazolium chloride (NBT) was used as a probe to determine the scavenging efficiency of •O2 −. Riboflavin may undergo photoreduction in the presence of oxidizing agents, and the reduced form is easily re-oxidized under aerobic conditions, producing •O2 −. This superoxide may transform nitrogen blue tetrazole chloride (NBT) into blue formazan, which displays peak absorbance at 560 nm. The test solution typically consists of riboflavin (6.67 μM), L-methionine (4.33 mM), NBT (25 μM), and SeCDs at different concentrations (0–100 μg·mL−1), in addition to PBS buffer (0.01 M, pH 7.4). A 30-watt light bulb was used to illuminate the combination for 300 s. A UV–Vis spectrophotometer was used to quantify the absorption of formazan. The elimination efficiency of •O2 − was determined using the formula: [(A1-A2)/(A1-A0)] × 100 %, where A0 signifies the absorbance of a solitary NBT, and A1 and A2 indicate the absorbance without SeCDs and with SeCDs, respectively.

20 μM Aβ42 was treated with different concentrations of SeCDs. After 5–10 min of incubation, the fluorescence intensity of each Aβ42 sample was measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer with an excitation wavelength set to 280 nm. The spectra were obtained within the wavelength range of 290 to 340 nm.

The ThT fluorescence assay was employed to assess the inhibitory effectiveness of SeCDs on Aβ42 fibrillation. A pH 7.4 PBS buffer solution containing 10 μg·mL−1 SeCDs was incubated with pre-treated Aβ42 at a concentration of 20 μM and Cu2+. To assess Aβ42 fibrillation, 20 μM ThT was incorporated into each sample. The ThT fluorescence at 485 nm was measured at various time intervals using excitation at 440 nm.

The CD spectra of Aβ (20 μM) treated with SeCDs were recorded using a Chirascan spectropolarimeter at 25 °C with a 10-mm path length. The trials were conducted at wavelengths from 190 to 260 nm, utilizing a bandwidth of 1.0 nm. The spectra for each sample were averaged across three scans.

The scavenging capability of SeCDs on Aβ42-induced intracellular ROS was detected by dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) assay with a ROS detection kit (Beyotime, S0033S). HT22 cells were pretreated with or without SeCDs. After the cell culture media were removed, DCFH-DA was added, and the cells were incubated in dark for 20 min. Finally, the stained cells were washed 3–5 times with PBS buffer, and the DCF fluorescence intensity at 525 nm was recorded under the excitation of 488 nm. At the same time, the fluorescence images and fluorescence intensity of the cells were obtained by CLSM and flow cytometry. HT22 cells treated with PBS buffer only were set as the negative control.

Xu Shao: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Tiange Fan: Software, Formal analysis, Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Chaoren Yan: Formal analysis, Methodology, Software. Xiuyun Cao: Software, Methodology, Formal analysis. Chao Wang: Writing – review & editing, Software, Methodology. Xin Wang: Writing – review & editing, Software, Methodology. Ping Guan: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Funding acquisition. Li Fan: Funding acquisition, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Xiaoling Hu: Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (82171962, and 82360368) and “Clinical medicine + Pharmacy” research center research fund of Air force medical university (LHJJ2023-YX05). This work was financially supported by the Key Research and Development Program of Shaanxi (2022GY-198, and 2023-YBGY-295).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2024.139333.
